    49→)
    50→
    51→const defaultExpectedTypeName = "<unspecified>"
    52→
    53→// We try to spread the load on apiserver by setting timeouts for
    54→// watch requests - it is random in [minWatchTimeout, 2*minWatchTimeout].
    55→var defaultMinWatchTimeout = 5 * time.Minute
    56→
    57→// ReflectorStore is the subset of cache.Store that the reflector uses
    58→type ReflectorStore interface {
    59→	// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    60→	Add(obj interface{}) error
    61→
    62→	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    63→	Update(obj interface{}) error
    64→
    65→	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    66→	Delete(obj interface{}) error
    67→
    68→	// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the
    69→	// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference
    70→	// it after calling this function.
    71→	Replace([]interface{}, string) error
    72→
    73→	// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has
    74→	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
    75→	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).
    76→	Resync() error
    77→}
    78→
    79→// TransformingStore is an optional interface that can be implemented by the provided store.
    80→// If implemented on the provided store reflector will use the same transformer in its internal stores.
    81→type TransformingStore interface {
    82→	ReflectorStore
    83→	Transformer() TransformFunc
    84→}
    85→
    86→// Reflector watches a specified resource and causes all changes to be reflected in the given store.
    87→type Reflector struct {
    88→	// name identifies this reflector. By default, it will be a file:line if possible.
    89→	name string
    90→	// The name of the type we expect to place in the store. The name
    91→	// will be the stringification of expectedGVK if provided, and the
    92→	// stringification of expectedType otherwise. It is for display
    93→	// only, and should not be used for parsing or comparison.
    94→	typeDescription string
    95→	// An example object of the type we expect to place in the store.
    96→	// Only the type needs to be right, except that when that is
    97→	// `unstructured.Unstructured` the object's `"apiVersion"` and
    98→	// `"kind"` must also be right.
    99→	expectedType reflect.Type
   100→	// The GVK of the object we expect to place in the store if unstructured.
   101→	expectedGVK *schema.GroupVersionKind
   102→	// The destination to sync up with the watch source
   103→	store ReflectorStore
   104→	// listerWatcher is used to perform lists and watches.
   105→	listerWatcher ListerWatcherWithContext
   106→	// backoff manages backoff of ListWatch
   107→	backoffManager wait.BackoffManager
   108→	resyncPeriod   time.Duration
   109→	// minWatchTimeout defines the minimum timeout for watch requests.
   110→	minWatchTimeout time.Duration
   111→	// clock allows tests to manipulate time
   112→	clock clock.Clock
   113→	// paginatedResult defines whether pagination should be forced for list calls.
   114→	// It is set based on the result of the initial list call.
   115→	paginatedResult bool
   116→	// lastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version token last
   117→	// observed when doing a sync with the underlying store
   118→	// it is thread safe, but not synchronized with the underlying store
   119→	lastSyncResourceVersion string
   120→	// isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable is true if the previous list or watch request with
   121→	// lastSyncResourceVersion failed with an "expired" or "too large resource version" error.
   122→	isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable bool
   123→	// lastSyncResourceVersionMutex guards read/write access to lastSyncResourceVersion
   124→	lastSyncResourceVersionMutex sync.RWMutex
   125→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
   126→	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
   127→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and resync watch lists.
   128→	// If unset, for consistent reads (RV="") or reads that opt-into arbitrarily old data
   129→	// (RV="0") it will default to pager.PageSize, for the rest (RV != "" && RV != "0")
   130→	// it will turn off pagination to allow serving them from watch cache.
   131→	// NOTE: It should be used carefully as paginated lists are always served directly from
   132→	// etcd, which is significantly less efficient and may lead to serious performance and
   133→	// scalability problems.
   134→	WatchListPageSize int64
   135→	// ShouldResync is invoked periodically and whenever it returns `true` the Store's Resync operation is invoked
   136→	ShouldResync func() bool
   137→	// MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration defines how long we should retry internal errors returned by watch.
   138→	MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration time.Duration
   139→	// useWatchList if turned on instructs the reflector to open a stream to bring data from the API server.
   140→	// Streaming has the primary advantage of using fewer server's resources to fetch data.
   141→	//
   142→	// The old behaviour establishes a LIST request which gets data in chunks.
   143→	// Paginated list is less efficient and depending on the actual size of objects
   144→	// might result in an increased memory consumption of the APIServer.
   145→	//
   146→	// See https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list#design-details
   147→	useWatchList bool
   148→}
   149→
   150→func (r *Reflector) Name() string {
   151→	return r.name
   152→}
   153→
   154→func (r *Reflector) TypeDescription() string {
   155→	return r.typeDescription
   156→}
   157→
   158→// ResourceVersionUpdater is an interface that allows store implementation to
   159→// track the current resource version of the reflector. This is especially
   160→// important if storage bookmarks are enabled.
   161→type ResourceVersionUpdater interface {
   162→	// UpdateResourceVersion is called each time current resource version of the reflector
   163→	// is updated.
   164→	UpdateResourceVersion(resourceVersion string)
   165→}
   166→
   167→// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
   168→// connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
   169→// will backoff and retry.
   170→//
   171→// The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
   172→// the error message at an appropriate level.
   173→//
   174→// Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
   175→// ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
   176→// should be offloaded.
   177→type WatchErrorHandler func(r *Reflector, err error)
   178→
   179→// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
   180→// connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
   181→// will backoff and retry.
   182→//
   183→// The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
   184→// the error message at an appropriate level.
   185→//
   186→// Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
   187→// ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
   188→// should be offloaded.
   189→type WatchErrorHandlerWithContext func(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error)
   190→
   191→// DefaultWatchErrorHandler is the default implementation of WatchErrorHandlerWithContext.
   192→func DefaultWatchErrorHandler(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error) {
   193→	switch {
   194→	case isExpiredError(err):
   195→		// Don't set LastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable - LIST call with ResourceVersion=RV already
   196→		// has a semantic that it returns data at least as fresh as provided RV.
   197→		// So first try to LIST with setting RV to resource version of last observed object.
   198→		klog.FromContext(ctx).V(4).Info("Watch closed", "reflector", r.name, "type", r.typeDescription, "err", err)
   199→	case err == io.EOF:
   200→		// watch closed normally

   102→	// The destination to sync up with the watch source
   103→	store ReflectorStore
   104→	// listerWatcher is used to perform lists and watches.
   105→	listerWatcher ListerWatcherWithContext
   106→	// backoff manages backoff of ListWatch
   107→	backoffManager wait.BackoffManager
   108→	resyncPeriod   time.Duration
   109→	// minWatchTimeout defines the minimum timeout for watch requests.
   110→	minWatchTimeout time.Duration
   111→	// clock allows tests to manipulate time
   112→	clock clock.Clock
   113→	// paginatedResult defines whether pagination should be forced for list calls.
   114→	// It is set based on the result of the initial list call.
   115→	paginatedResult bool
   116→	// lastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version token last
   117→	// observed when doing a sync with the underlying store
   118→	// it is thread safe, but not synchronized with the underlying store
   119→	lastSyncResourceVersion string
   120→	// isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable is true if the previous list or watch request with
   121→	// lastSyncResourceVersion failed with an "expired" or "too large resource version" error.
   122→	isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable bool
   123→	// lastSyncResourceVersionMutex guards read/write access to lastSyncResourceVersion
   124→	lastSyncResourceVersionMutex sync.RWMutex
   125→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
   126→	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
   127→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and resync watch lists.
   128→	// If unset, for consistent reads (RV="") or reads that opt-into arbitrarily old data
   129→	// (RV="0") it will default to pager.PageSize, for the rest (RV != "" && RV != "0")
   130→	// it will turn off pagination to allow serving them from watch cache.
   131→	// NOTE: It should be used carefully as paginated lists are always served directly from
   132→	// etcd, which is significantly less efficient and may lead to serious performance and
   133→	// scalability problems.
   134→	WatchListPageSize int64
   135→	// ShouldResync is invoked periodically and whenever it returns `true` the Store's Resync operation is invoked
   136→	ShouldResync func() bool
   137→	// MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration defines how long we should retry internal errors returned by watch.
   138→	MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration time.Duration
   139→	// useWatchList if turned on instructs the reflector to open a stream to bring data from the API server.
   140→	// Streaming has the primary advantage of using fewer server's resources to fetch data.
   141→	//
   142→	// The old behaviour establishes a LIST request which gets data in chunks.
   143→	// Paginated list is less efficient and depending on the actual size of objects
   144→	// might result in an increased memory consumption of the APIServer.
   145→	//
   146→	// See https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list#design-details
   147→	useWatchList bool
   148→}

   150→func (r *Reflector) Name() string {
   151→	return r.name
   152→}
   153→
   154→func (r *Reflector) TypeDescription() string {
   155→	return r.typeDescription
   156→}
   157→
   158→// ResourceVersionUpdater is an interface that allows store implementation to
   159→// track the current resource version of the reflector. This is especially
   160→// important if storage bookmarks are enabled.
   161→type ResourceVersionUpdater interface {
   162→	// UpdateResourceVersion is called each time current resource version of the reflector
   163→	// is updated.
   164→	UpdateResourceVersion(resourceVersion string)
   165→}
   166→
   167→// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
   168→// connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
   169→// will backoff and retry.
   170→//
   171→// The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
   172→// the error message at an appropriate level.
   173→//
   174→// Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
   175→// ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
   176→// should be offloaded.
   177→type WatchErrorHandler func(r *Reflector, err error)
   178→
   179→// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
   180→// connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
   181→// will backoff and retry.
   182→//
   183→// The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
   184→// the error message at an appropriate level.
   185→//
   186→// Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
   187→// ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
   188→// should be offloaded.
   189→type WatchErrorHandlerWithContext func(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error)
   190→
   191→// DefaultWatchErrorHandler is the default implementation of WatchErrorHandlerWithContext.
   192→func DefaultWatchErrorHandler(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error) {
   193→	switch {
   194→	case isExpiredError(err):
   195→		// Don't set LastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable - LIST call with ResourceVersion=RV already
   196→		// has a semantic that it returns data at least as fresh as provided RV.
   197→		// So first try to LIST with setting RV to resource version of last observed object.
   198→		klog.FromContext(ctx).V(4).Info("Watch closed", "reflector", r.name, "type", r.typeDescription, "err", err)
   199→	case err == io.EOF:
   200→		// watch closed normally

    57→// ReflectorStore is the subset of cache.Store that the reflector uses
    58→type ReflectorStore interface {

    60→	Add(obj interface{}) error
    61→
    62→	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    63→	Update(obj interface{}) error
    64→
    65→	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key

    70→	// it after calling this function.

    72→

    74→	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
    75→	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).

   101→	expectedGVK *schema.GroupVersionKind

   106→	// backoff manages backoff of ListWatch

   118→	// it is thread safe, but not synchronized with the underlying store
   119→	lastSyncResourceVersion string
   120→	// isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable is true if the previous list or watch request with

   122→	isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable bool
   123→	// lastSyncResourceVersionMutex guards read/write access to lastSyncResourceVersion
   124→	lastSyncResourceVersionMutex sync.RWMutex
   125→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
   126→	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
   127→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and resync watch lists.
   128→	// If unset, for consistent reads (RV="") or reads that opt-into arbitrarily old data
   129→	// (RV="0") it will default to pager.PageSize, for the rest (RV != "" && RV != "0")
   130→	// it will turn off pagination to allow serving them from watch cache.
   131→	// NOTE: It should be used carefully as paginated lists are always served directly from

   135→	// ShouldResync is invoked periodically and whenever it returns `true` the Store's Resync operation is invoked
   136→	ShouldResync func() bool
   137→	// MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration defines how long we should retry internal errors returned by watch.
   138→	MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration time.Duration
   139→	// useWatchList if turned on instructs the reflector to open a stream to bring data from the API server.
   140→	// Streaming has the primary advantage of using fewer server's resources to fetch data.

1→/*
     2→Copyright 2014 The Kubernetes Authors.

    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→*/
    16→
    17→package cache
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"errors"
    21→	"fmt"
    22→	"sync"
    23→	"time"
    24→
    25→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"
    26→
    27→	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
    28→	utiltrace "k8s.io/utils/trace"
    29→)
    30→
    31→// DeltaFIFOOptions is the configuration parameters for DeltaFIFO. All are
    32→// optional.
    33→type DeltaFIFOOptions struct {
    34→
    35→	// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's
    36→	// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional
    37→	// handling around deleted objects and queue state).
    38→	// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.
    39→	KeyFunction KeyFunc
    40→
    41→	// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of
    42→	// this queue "knows about". It is used to decide which items are missing
    43→	// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.
    44→	// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().
    45→	KnownObjects KeyListerGetter
    46→
    47→	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer
    48→	// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was
    49→	// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For
    50→	// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.
    51→	// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.
    52→	// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.
    53→	EmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
    54→
    55→	// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please
    56→	// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.
    57→	Transformer TransformFunc
    58→
    59→	// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().
    60→	Logger *klog.Logger
    61→}
    62→
    63→// DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the
    64→// accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object
    65→// but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that
    66→// object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta
    67→// except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the
    68→// Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does
    69→// not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new
    70→// Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a
    71→// DeletedFinalStateUnknown.
    72→//
    73→// The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that
    74→// an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.
    75→// If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in
    76→// replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic
    77→// resync events.
    78→//
    79→// DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is
    80→// intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls
    81→// the Pop() method.
    82→//
    83→// DeltaFIFO solves this use case:
    84→//   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.
    85→//   - When you process an object, you want to see everything
    86→//     that's happened to it since you last processed it.
    87→//   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.
    88→//   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.
    89→//
    90→// DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return
    91→// interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they
    92→// will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns
    93→// the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.
    94→//
    95→// A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities
    96→// to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in
    97→// question are called "known objects" and this set of objects
    98→// modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods
    99→// (each in a different way).
   100→//
   101→// A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple
   102→// threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly
   103→// different versions of the same object.
   104→type DeltaFIFO struct {
   105→	// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.
   106→	lock sync.RWMutex
   107→	cond sync.Cond
   108→
   109→	// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.
   110→	// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.
   111→	items map[string]Deltas
   112→
   113→	// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().
   114→	// There are no duplicates in `queue`.
   115→	// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.
   116→	queue []string
   117→
   118→	// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated
   119→	// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.
   120→	populated bool
   121→	// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()
   122→	initialPopulationCount int
   123→
   124→	// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item
   125→	// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.
   126→	keyFunc KeyFunc
   127→
   128→	// knownObjects list keys that are "known" --- affecting Delete(),
   129→	// Replace(), and Resync()
   130→	knownObjects KeyListerGetter
   131→
   132→	// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.
   133→	// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.
   134→	closed bool
   135→
   136→	// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync
   137→	// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).
   138→	emitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
   139→
   140→	// Called with every object if non-nil.
   141→	transformer TransformFunc
   142→
   143→	// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing
   144→	// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.
   145→	logger klog.Logger
   146→}
   147→
   148→// TransformFunc allows for transforming an object before it will be processed.
   149→//
   150→// The most common usage pattern is to clean-up some parts of the object to
   151→// reduce component memory usage if a given component doesn't care about them.
   152→//
   153→// New in v1.27: TransformFunc sees the object before any other actor, and it
   154→// is now safe to mutate the object in place instead of making a copy.
   155→//
   156→// It's recommended for the TransformFunc to be idempotent.
   157→// It MUST be idempotent if objects already present in the cache are passed to
   158→// the Replace() to avoid re-mutating them. Default informers do not pass
   159→// existing objects to Replace though.
   160→//
   161→// Note that TransformFunc is called while inserting objects into the
   162→// notification queue and is therefore extremely performance sensitive; please
   163→// do not do anything that will take a long time.
   164→type TransformFunc func(interface{}) (interface{}, error)
   165→
   166→// DeltaType is the type of a change (addition, deletion, etc)
   167→type DeltaType string
   168→
   169→// Change type definition
   170→const (
   171→	Added   DeltaType = "Added"
   172→	Updated DeltaType = "Updated"
   173→	Deleted DeltaType = "Deleted"
   174→	// Replaced is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do a
   175→	// relist, or on initial listing of objects. We don't know if the replaced
   176→	// object has changed.
   177→	//
   178→	// NOTE: Previous versions of DeltaFIFO would use Sync for Replace events
   179→	// as well. Hence, Replaced is only emitted when the option
   180→	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is true.
   181→	Replaced DeltaType = "Replaced"
   182→	// ReplacedAll is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do
   183→	// a relist, or on initial listing of objects. This is the same reason as
   184→	// Replaced but will be emitted instead when the FIFO supports atomic
   185→	// replacement. This event will return the full list of replaced items
   186→	// instead of a single object.
   187→	ReplacedAll DeltaType = "ReplacedAll"
   188→	// Sync is for synthetic events during a periodic resync.
   189→	Sync DeltaType = "Sync"
   190→	// SyncAll indicates all known objects should be reprocessed.
   191→	// This event contains an object of type SyncAllInfo.
   192→	SyncAll DeltaType = "SyncAll"
   193→)
   194→
   195→// Delta is a member of Deltas (a list of Delta objects) which
   196→// in its turn is the type stored by a DeltaFIFO. It tells you what
   197→// change happened, and the object's state after* that change.
   198→//
   199→// [*] Unless the change is a deletion, and then you'll get the final
   200→// state of the object before it was deleted.

All are\n// optional.\ntype DeltaFIFOOptions struct {\n\n\t// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's\n\t// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional\n\t// handling around deleted objects and queue state).\n\t// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.\n\tKeyFunction KeyFunc\n\n\t// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of\n\t// this queue \"knows about\". It is used to decide which items are missing\n\t// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.\n\t// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().\n\tKnownObjects KeyListerGetter\n\n\t// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer\n\t// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was\n\t// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For\n\t// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.\n\t// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.\n\t// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.\n\tEmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool\n\n\t// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please\n\t// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.\n\tTransformer TransformFunc\n\n\t// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().\n\tLogger *klog.Logger\n}\n\n// DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the\n// accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object\n// but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that\n// object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta\n// except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the\n// Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does\n// not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new\n// Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a\n// DeletedFinalStateUnknown.\n//\n// The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that\n// an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.\n// If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in\n// replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic\n// resync events.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is\n// intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls\n// the Pop() method.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO solves this use case:\n//   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.\n//   - When you process an object, you want to see everything\n//     that's happened to it since you last processed it.\n//   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.\n//   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return\n// interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they\n// will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns\n// the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.\n//\n// A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities\n// to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in\n// question are called \"known objects\" and this set of objects\n// modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods\n// (each in a different way).\n//\n// A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple\n// threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly\n// different versions of the same object.\ntype DeltaFIFO struct {\n\t// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.\n\tlock sync.RWMutex\n\tcond sync.Cond\n\n\t// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.\n\t// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.\n\titems map[string]Deltas\n\n\t// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().\n\t// There are no duplicates in `queue`.\n\t// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.\n\tqueue []string\n\n\t// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated\n\t// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.\n\tpopulated bool\n\t// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()\n\tinitialPopulationCount int\n\n\t// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item\n\t// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.\n\tkeyFunc KeyFunc\n\n\t// knownObjects list keys that are \"known\" --- affecting Delete(),\n\t// Replace(), and Resync()\n\tknownObjects KeyListerGetter\n\n\t// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.\n\t// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.\n\tclosed bool\n\n\t// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync\n\t// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).\n\temitDeltaTypeReplaced bool\n\n\t// Called with every object if non-nil.\n\ttransformer TransformFunc\n\n\t// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing\n\t// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.\n\tlogger klog.Logger\n}\n\n// TransformFunc allows for transforming an object before it will be processed.\n//\n// The most common usage pattern is to clean-up some parts of the object to\n// reduce component memory usage if a given component doesn't care about them.\n//\n// New in v1.27: TransformFunc sees the object before any other actor, and it\n// is now safe to mutate the object in place instead of making a copy.\n//\n// It's recommended for the TransformFunc to be idempotent.\n// It MUST be idempotent if objects already present in the cache are passed to\n// the Replace() to avoid re-mutating them. Default informers do not pass\n// existing objects to Replace though.\n//\n// Note that TransformFunc is called while inserting objects into the\n// notification queue and is therefore extremely performance sensitive; please\n// do not do anything that will take a long time.\ntype TransformFunc func(interface{}) (interface{}, error)\n\n// DeltaType is the type of a change (addition, deletion, etc)\ntype DeltaType string\n\n// Change type definition\nconst (\n\tAdded   DeltaType = \"Added\"\n\tUpdated DeltaType = \"Updated\"\n\tDeleted DeltaType = \"Deleted\"\n\t// Replaced is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do a\n\t// relist, or on initial listing of objects. We don't know if the replaced\n\t// object has changed.\n\t//\n\t// NOTE: Previous versions of DeltaFIFO would use Sync for Replace events\n\t// as well. Hence, Replaced is only emitted when the option\n\t// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is true.\n\tReplaced DeltaType = \"Replaced\"\n\t// ReplacedAll is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do\n\t// a relist, or on initial listing of objects. This is the same reason as\n\t// Replaced but will be emitted instead when the FIFO supports atomic\n\t// replacement. This event will return the full list of replaced items\n\t// instead of a single object.\n\tReplacedAll DeltaType = \"ReplacedAll\"\n\t// Sync is for synthetic events during a periodic resync.\n\tSync DeltaType = \"Sync\"\n\t// SyncAll indicates all known objects should be reprocessed.\n\t// This event contains an object of type SyncAllInfo.\n\tSyncAll DeltaType = \"SyncAll\"\n)\n\n// Delta is a member of Deltas (a list of Delta objects) which\n// in its turn is the type stored by a DeltaFIFO. It tells you what\n// change happened, and the object's state after* that change.\n//\n// [*] Unless the change is a deletion, and then you'll get the final\n// state of the object before it was deleted.", "numLines": 200, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 746}}

    31→// DeltaFIFOOptions is the configuration parameters for DeltaFIFO. All are
    32→// optional.

    34→
    35→	// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's
    36→	// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional
    37→	// handling around deleted objects and queue state).
    38→	// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.
    39→	KeyFunction KeyFunc
    40→
    41→	// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of
    42→	// this queue "knows about". It is used to decide which items are missing
    43→	// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.
    44→	// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().

    46→

    48→	// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was
    49→	// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For
    50→	// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.

    56→	// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.

    58→
    59→	// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().
    60→	Logger *klog.Logger
    61→}
    62→
    63→// DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the
    64→// accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object
    65→// but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that
    66→// object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta
    67→// except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the
    68→// Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does
    69→// not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new
    70→// Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a
    71→// DeletedFinalStateUnknown.
    72→//
    73→// The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that
    74→// an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.
    75→// If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in
    76→// replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic
    77→// resync events.
    78→//
    79→// DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is
    80→// intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls
    81→// the Pop() method.
    82→//
    83→// DeltaFIFO solves this use case:
    84→//   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.
    85→//   - When you process an object, you want to see everything
    86→//     that's happened to it since you last processed it.
    87→//   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.
    88→//   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.

    90→// DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return
    91→// interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they
    92→// will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns
    93→// the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.
    94→//
    95→// A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities
    96→// to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in
    97→// question are called "known objects" and this set of objects
    98→// modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods
    99→// (each in a different way).
   100→//
   101→// A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple
   102→// threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly
   103→// different versions of the same object.
   104→type DeltaFIFO struct {
   105→	// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.
   106→	lock sync.RWMutex
   107→	cond sync.Cond
   108→
   109→	// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.
   110→	// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.
   111→	items map[string]Deltas
   112→
   113→	// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().
   114→	// There are no duplicates in `queue`.
   115→	// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.
   116→	queue []string
   117→
   118→	// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated
   119→	// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.
   120→	populated bool
   121→	// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()
   122→	initialPopulationCount int
   123→
   124→	// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item
   125→	// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.
   126→	keyFunc KeyFunc
   127→
   128→	// knownObjects list keys that are "known" --- affecting Delete(),
   129→	// Replace(), and Resync()
   130→	knownObjects KeyListerGetter
   131→
   132→	// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.
   133→	// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.
   134→	closed bool
   135→
   136→	// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync
   137→	// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).
   138→	emitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
   139→
   140→	// Called with every object if non-nil.
   141→	transformer TransformFunc
   142→
   143→	// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing
   144→	// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.
   145→	logger klog.Logger
   146→}
   147→
   148→// TransformFunc allows for transforming an object before it will be processed.
   149→//
   150→// The most common usage pattern is to clean-up some parts of the object to
   151→// reduce component memory usage if a given component doesn't care about them.
   152→//
   153→// New in v1.27: TransformFunc sees the object before any other actor, and it
   154→// is now safe to mutate the object in place instead of making a copy.
   155→//
   156→// It's recommended for the TransformFunc to be idempotent.
   157→// It MUST be idempotent if objects already present in the cache are passed to
   158→// the Replace() to avoid re-mutating them. Default informers do not pass
   159→// existing objects to Replace though.
   160→//
   161→// Note that TransformFunc is called while inserting objects into the
   162→// notification queue and is therefore extremely performance sensitive; please
   163→// do not do anything that will take a long time.
   164→type TransformFunc func(interface{}) (interface{}, error)
   165→
   166→// DeltaType is the type of a change (addition, deletion, etc)
   167→type DeltaType string
   168→
   169→// Change type definition
   170→const (
   171→	Added   DeltaType = "Added"
   172→	Updated DeltaType = "Updated"
   173→	Deleted DeltaType = "Deleted"
   174→	// Replaced is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do a
   175→	// relist, or on initial listing of objects. We don't know if the replaced
   176→	// object has changed.
   177→	//
   178→	// NOTE: Previous versions of DeltaFIFO would use Sync for Replace events
   179→	// as well. Hence, Replaced is only emitted when the option
   180→	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is true.
   181→	Replaced DeltaType = "Replaced"

   183→	// a relist, or on initial listing of objects. This is the same reason as
   184→	// Replaced but will be emitted instead when the FIFO supports atomic
   185→	// replacement. This event will return the full list of replaced items
   186→	// instead of a single object.
   187→	ReplacedAll DeltaType = "ReplacedAll"

   189→	Sync DeltaType = "Sync"
   190→	// SyncAll indicates all known objects should be reprocessed.
   191→	// This event contains an object of type SyncAllInfo.
   192→	SyncAll DeltaType = "SyncAll"
   193→)

   195→// Delta is a member of Deltas (a list of Delta objects) which
   196→// in its turn is the type stored by a DeltaFIFO. It tells you what
   197→// change happened, and the object's state after* that change.
   198→//
   199→// [*] Unless the change is a deletion, and then you'll get the final
   200→// state of the object before it was deleted.

   182→	// ReplacedAll is emitted when we encountered watch errors and had to do
   183→	// a relist, or on initial listing of objects. This is the same reason as
   184→	// Replaced but will be emitted instead when the FIFO supports atomic
   185→	// replacement. This event will return the full list of replaced items
   186→	// instead of a single object.
   187→	ReplacedAll DeltaType = "ReplacedAll"
   188→	// Sync is for synthetic events during a periodic resync.
   189→	Sync DeltaType = "Sync"
   190→	// SyncAll indicates all known objects should be reprocessed.
   191→	// This event contains an object of type SyncAllInfo.
   192→	SyncAll DeltaType = "SyncAll"
   193→)
   194→
   195→// Delta is a member of Deltas (a list of Delta objects) which
   196→// in its turn is the type stored by a DeltaFIFO. It tells you what
   197→// change happened, and the object's state after* that change.
   198→//
   199→// [*] Unless the change is a deletion, and then you'll get the final
   200→// state of the object before it was deleted.

    16→
    17→package cache
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"context"
    21→	"errors"
    22→	"fmt"
    23→	"sync"
    24→	"time"
    25→
    26→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
    27→	utilruntime "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
    28→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"
    29→	clientgofeaturegate "k8s.io/client-go/features"
    30→	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
    31→)
    32→
    33→// This file implements a low-level controller that is used in
    34→// sharedIndexInformer, which is an implementation of
    35→// SharedIndexInformer.  Such informers, in turn, are key components
    36→// in the high level controllers that form the backbone of the
    37→// Kubernetes control plane.  Look at those for examples, or the
    38→// example in
    39→// https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/tree/master/examples/workqueue
    40→// .
    41→
    42→// Config contains all the settings for one of these low-level controllers.
    43→type Config struct {
    44→	// The queue for your objects - has to be a DeltaFIFO due to
    45→	// assumptions in the implementation. Your Process() function
    46→	// should accept the output of this Queue's Pop() method.
    47→	Queue
    48→
    49→	// Something that can list and watch your objects.
    50→	ListerWatcher
    51→
    52→	// Process can process a popped Deltas.
    53→	Process ProcessFunc
    54→
    55→	// ProcessBatch can process a batch of popped Deltas, which should return `TransactionError` if not all items
    56→	// in the batch were successfully processed.
    57→	//
    58→	// For batch processing to be used:
    59→	// * ProcessBatch must be non-nil
    60→	// * Queue must implement QueueWithBatch
    61→	// * The client InOrderInformersBatchProcess feature gate must be enabled
    62→	//
    63→	// If any of those are false, Process is used and no batch processing is done.
    64→	ProcessBatch ProcessBatchFunc
    65→
    66→	// ObjectType is an example object of the type this controller is
    67→	// expected to handle.
    68→	ObjectType runtime.Object
    69→
    70→	// ObjectDescription is the description to use when logging type-specific information about this controller.
    71→	ObjectDescription string
    72→
    73→	// FullResyncPeriod is the period at which ShouldResync is considered.
    74→	FullResyncPeriod time.Duration
    75→
    76→	// MinWatchTimeout, if set, will define the minimum timeout for watch requests send
    77→	// to kube-apiserver. However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid
    78→	// negative performance impact on controlplane.
    79→	// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.
    80→	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
    81→
    82→	// ShouldResync is periodically used by the reflector to determine
    83→	// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or
    84→	// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the
    85→	// resync.
    86→	ShouldResync ShouldResyncFunc
    87→
    88→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
    89→	//
    90→	// Contextual logging: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of WatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
    91→	WatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandler
    92→
    93→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error
    94→	// and WatchErrorHandler is not set.
    95→	WatchErrorHandlerWithContext WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
    96→
    97→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.
    98→	WatchListPageSize int64
    99→}

    46→func Example_inClusterConfiguration() {
    47→	// This example demonstrates how to create a clientset for an application
    48→	// running inside a Kubernetes cluster. It uses the pod's service account
    49→	// for authentication.
    50→
    51→	// rest.InClusterConfig() returns a configuration object that can be used to
    52→	// create a clientset. It is the recommended way to configure a client for
    53→	// in-cluster applications.
    54→	config, err := rest.InClusterConfig()
    55→	if err != nil {
    56→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
    57→		return
    58→	}
    59→
    60→	// Create the clientset.
    61→	clientset, err := kubernetes.NewForConfig(config)
    62→	if err != nil {
    63→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
    64→		return
    65→	}
    66→
    67→	// Use the clientset to interact with the API.
    68→
    69→	pods, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").List(context.TODO(), metav1.ListOptions{})
    70→	if err != nil {
    71→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
    72→		return
    73→	}
    74→
    75→	fmt.Printf("There are %d pods in the default namespace\n", len(pods.Items))
    76→}
    77→
    78→func Example_outOfClusterConfiguration() {
    79→	// This example demonstrates how to create a clientset for an application
    80→	// running outside of a Kubernetes cluster, using a kubeconfig file. This is
    81→	// the standard approach for local development and command-line tools.
    82→
    83→	// The default location for the kubeconfig file is in the user's home directory.
    84→	var kubeconfig string
    85→	if home := os.Getenv("HOME"); home != "" {
    86→		kubeconfig = filepath.Join(home, ".kube", "config")
    87→	}
    88→
    89→	// Create the client configuration from the kubeconfig file.
    90→	config, err := clientcmd.BuildConfigFromFlags("", kubeconfig)
    91→	if err != nil {
    92→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
    93→		return
    94→	}
    95→
    96→	// Configure client-side rate limiting.
    97→	config.QPS = 50
    98→	config.Burst = 100
    99→
   100→	// A clientset contains clients for all the API groups and versions supported
   101→	// by the cluster.
   102→	clientset, err := kubernetes.NewForConfig(config)
   103→	if err != nil {
   104→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
   105→		return
   106→	}
   107→
   108→	// Use the clientset to interact with the API.
   109→
   110→	pods, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").List(context.TODO(), metav1.ListOptions{})
   111→	if err != nil {
   112→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
   113→		return
   114→	}
   115→
   116→	fmt.Printf("There are %d pods in the default namespace\n", len(pods.Items))
   117→}

   119→func Example_handlingAPIErrors() {
   120→	// This example demonstrates how to handle common API errors.
   121→	// Create a NotFound error to simulate a failed API request.
   122→	err := errors.NewNotFound(schema.GroupResource{Group: "v1", Resource: "pods"}, "my-pod")
   123→
   124→	// The errors.IsNotFound() function checks if an error is a NotFound error.
   125→	if errors.IsNotFound(err) {
   126→		fmt.Println("Pod not found")
   127→	}
   128→
   129→	// The errors package provides functions for other common API errors, such as:
   130→	// - errors.IsAlreadyExists(err)
   131→	// - errors.IsConflict(err)
   132→	// - errors.IsServerTimeout(err)
   133→
   134→	// Output:
   135→	// Pod not found
   136→}
   137→
   138→func Example_usingDynamicClient() {
   139→	// This example demonstrates how to create and use a dynamic client to work
   140→	// with Custom Resources or other objects without needing their Go type
   141→	// definitions.
   142→
   143→	// Configure the client (out-of-cluster for this example).
   144→	var kubeconfig string
   145→	if home := os.Getenv("HOME"); home != "" {
   146→		kubeconfig = filepath.Join(home, ".kube", "config")
   147→	}
   148→	config, err := clientcmd.BuildConfigFromFlags("", kubeconfig)
   149→	if err != nil {
   150→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
   151→		return
   152→	}
   153→
   154→	// Create a dynamic client.
   155→	dynamicClient, err := dynamic.NewForConfig(config)
   156→	if err != nil {
   157→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
   158→		return
   159→	}
   160→
   161→	// Define the GroupVersionResource for the object you want to access.
   162→	// For Pods, this is {Group: "", Version: "v1", Resource: "pods"}.
   163→	gvr := schema.GroupVersionResource{Version: "v1", Resource: "pods"}
   164→
   165→	// Use the dynamic client to list all pods in the "default" namespace.
   166→	// The result is an UnstructuredList.
   167→	list, err := dynamicClient.Resource(gvr).Namespace("default").List(context.TODO(), metav1.ListOptions{})
   168→	if err != nil {
   169→		fmt.Printf("Error encountered: %v\n", err)
   170→		return
   171→	}
   172→
   173→	// Iterate over the list and print the name of each pod.
   174→	for _, item := range list.Items {
   175→		name, found, err := unstructured.NestedString(item.Object, "metadata", "name")
   176→		if err != nil || !found {
   177→			fmt.Printf("Could not find name for pod: %v\n", err)
   178→			continue
   179→		}
   180→		fmt.Printf("Pod Name: %s\n", name)
   181→	}
   182→}
